prompt: How does the river Nile come to flood in summer when other rivers fall?
text: The Etesian Winds are the cause. They blow continually from the North and stop at about the summer solstice; then blowing for so much longer a time than any other winds they are known to do, they make up their deficiency by being constantly in full blast. If therefore these winds fail to come when expected, the Nile does not flood at all as it should.

prompt: Why do you say that custom is king over all?
text: To me it seems to be so; for I have myself had intercourse with kings, and among others also Croesus the Lydian, who was greatly superior to other kings both in happiness of fortune and also in magnificence of wealth: now this man, when he was at his height of prosperity, was overcome by custom; and being overcome by it he became a slave instead of remaining free.

prompt: How do the Babylonians give their daughters in marriage?
text: The women among the Babylonians are wont to marry as follows: when a girl has come to be of age for marriage, her parents summon an assembly of all who dwell about them and bid them choose out each one of them which man he most likes; and when they have gone through them all and declared their opinions, it is necessary that the parents do whatsoever shall have been voted by the greatest number.

prompt: Tell me of the gold-digging ants of India, larger than foxes.
text: O ye men of Hellas! I know that there are a race called Ants, and their size is great; but whether they be so large as to have been called from this name by the Indians, namely "ants," that I am not able to say. For I heard from them nothing but marvels besides all these which they told about the gold-digging ants.

prompt: Tell me of the Egyptians and how their customs differ from those of other men.
text: Stranger to Hellas, this race has been by nature most prone to novelty of any nation that I know; for when they have heard but a word or seen it but once, straightway they forthwith are acquainted with all. Whenever any new thing comes in from abroad they are more eager than any other nation to learn about it; but when the thing has been learnt by them, forthwith they lay aside all eagerness to know and do not care at all.
